The apostle continues with the duty of masters to their servants. Not only justice is required of them, but strict fairness and kindness. Let them deal with servants as they expect God should deal with themselves.

No duties can be done correctly unless we persevere in fervent prayer and watch therein with thanksgiving. The people are to pray particularly for their ministers. Believers are encouraged to act righteously toward unbelievers. Be careful in all conversations with them, to do them good, and recommend religion by all suitable means. Diligence in using time well commends religion to the good opinion of others. Even what is only carelessness may cause a lasting prejudice against the truth. Let all discourse be discreet and appropriate, as becomes Christians. Though it may not always be from grace, it must always be with grace. Though our conversation is about common things, yet it must be in a Christian manner. Grace is the salt which seasons our conversation and keeps it from corrupting. It is not enough to answer what is asked unless we answer correctly also.

Ministers are servants to Christ and fellow-servants to one another. They have one Lord, though they have different positions and abilities for service. It is a great comfort under the troubles and difficulties of life to have fellow Christians caring for us. Circumstances of life make no difference in the spiritual relationship among sincere Christians; they share the same privileges and are entitled to the same regard. What amazing changes Divine grace makes! Unfaithful servants become faithful and beloved brethren, and some who had done wrong become fellow-workers of good.

Paul had disagreed with Barnabas on account of Mark, yet he is not only reconciled but recommends him to the churches; an example of a truly Christian and forgiving spirit. If people have been guilty of a fault, it must not always be remembered against them. We must forget as well as forgive. The apostle had comfort in the fellowship of saints and ministers. One is his fellow-servant, another his fellow-prisoner, and all are his fellow-workers, working out their own salvation and endeavoring to promote the salvation of others. Effective, fervent prayer is a prevailing prayer and avails much. The smiles, flatteries, or frowns of the world, the spirit of error, or the working of self-love leads many to a way of preaching and living which falls far short of fulfilling their ministry. But those who preach the same doctrine as Paul and follow his example may expect Divine favor and blessing.
